title: Projects
text:
This page presents selected projects that reflect my development in cybersecurity, privacy, governance, and business analysis. These examples combine technical understanding with structured communication and practical recommendations.




This project involved cybersecurity assessment activities aligned with a recognized control framework in a corporate environment.



I supported audit-related activities, reviewed materials and evidence, and contributed to the organization of findings and reporting.




NIST
Control review
Evidence analysis
Stakeholder communication





Strengthened my understanding of audit-based cybersecurity evaluation.
Improved my ability to organize findings into practical communication.
Reinforced the importance of documentation quality and control maturity.








This project focused on external cybersecurity audit support in an operationally important infrastructure environment.



I assisted with assessment activities, documentation review, and coordination of evidence and observations.




NIST
Governance review
Risk-based observation
Reporting support





Developed stronger attention to evidence quality and consistency.
Learned how cybersecurity controls relate to operational continuity.
Improved analytical communication in structured audit settings.








This work related to third-party risk and the need to assess vendors and external partners from a governance and security perspective.



I contributed to risk evaluation activities, evidence review, and structured documentation for third-party assessment processes.




TPRM
Vendor risk review
Documentation analysis
Risk communication





Increased my understanding of supply-chain and vendor-related cyber risk.
Improved my ability to communicate control concerns in a business context.
Reinforced the value of standardized assessment processes.








This project involved data protection and privacy-related governance activities connected to compliance obligations.



I supported privacy-related assessment and governance work, including documentation and structured review of compliance practices.




LGPD
Privacy governance
Documentation review
Compliance support





Expanded my practical understanding of privacy governance.
Improved my ability to connect legal requirements with operational practices.
Strengthened my interest in privacy and data governance work.








As part of my MBA, I worked on projects involving strategic analysis, business frameworks, and structured recommendations.



I applied academic frameworks to evaluate business environments, identify challenges, and propose practical actions.




SWOT
PESTEL
Porter’s Five Forces
VRIO
Value Chain





Improved my ability to transform analysis into business recommendations.
Strengthened executive-style writing and presentation skills.
Learned to balance theory with practical decision-making.

text:
The following site map was generated with LLM assistance for Assignment 3:

Home / About
Resume
Projects / Case Studies
Skills & Certifications
Contact



I kept this structure because it aligns with the professor’s examples and creates a clear separation between identity, experience, projects, and skills.







The Home page was drafted to introduce my profile, present a short value proposition, and provide navigation into the rest of the portfolio.




Replaced generic MBA wording with my actual focus on cybersecurity, governance, risk, and privacy.
Kept the strengths / focus areas / availability structure from my Phase 1 portfolio.
Simplified wording to sound more natural and professional.








The Resume page was drafted to expand my education, certifications, and professional experience into a more structured format.




Adapted the professional summary to reflect my real interests and experience.
Removed overly generic job language.
Focused on audit, governance, privacy, and risk-related work.








The Projects page was drafted to transform short project references into mini case studies.




Selected only projects relevant to my portfolio theme.
Rewrote sections to better reflect what I actually did and learned.
Used consistent headings for context, role, tools, and takeaways.








The Skills page was drafted to group my competencies into clearer categories.




Replaced generic headings with more professional categories.
Kept only frameworks and tools that I have actually studied or used.
Aligned the page with the same professional identity used across the website.






The LLM helped me brainstorm structure and produce initial draft text. My main role was to review the output, remove generic statements, verify alignment with the assignment, and rewrite sections in a more authentic and accurate way. I also used my Phase 1 submission as a reference point so that the Phase 2 portfolio would feel like a true expansion rather than a completely new version.

title: Home
text:
About Me
I am building my career at the intersection of business and cybersecurity. My background includes work related to information security, third-party risk, privacy, and compliance, supported by hands-on experience with frameworks and practices such as ISO/IEC 27001, NIST, LGPD, GDPR, and TPRM. Through my MBA, I am expanding that technical foundation with stronger business analysis, strategy, and communication skills.

title: Reflection
text:
Reflection on My Use of LLMs in Portfolio Development
In the first phase of this project, I used an LLM mainly to generate a one-page portfolio outline and sample text. That support was useful because it gave me a starting structure and helped me organize essential sections such as the header, about section, skills, education, experience, and projects. However, the one-page format required me to be very selective. I needed to streamline the generated content and adapt it to a cleaner, more personal layout.
In this second phase, the use of the LLM became more helpful because the assignment required a multi-page website. Expanding the site into multiple pages made it easier to separate content by purpose. Instead of compressing everything into one page, I could organize my portfolio more clearly into Home, Resume, Projects, Skills, and Contact. This structure improved readability and made the site feel more professional and easier to navigate. The LLM was particularly useful for brainstorming the site map, suggesting page-by-page content distribution, and helping me draft section headings and short descriptions.
At the same time, I needed to ensure the portfolio remained authentic and accurate. I reviewed all generated text critically and replaced generic phrases with content that reflected my real background, academic journey, and career interests. I kept only the sections that matched my actual experience and removed anything that sounded too broad or unrealistic. I also checked assignment instructions carefully to make sure the final structure met the professor’s requirements for navigation, multiple pages, reflection, and appendix documentation.
Overall, this process showed me that LLMs are most useful when they support structure, drafting, and brainstorming, but not when they are used without review. The final quality of the portfolio depended on my own judgment, editing, and personalization. I see the LLM as a productivity tool that helped me move faster, while my role was to verify, refine, and humanize the final result.
